content.problemStatement:
1NF is all about making your data atomic - which means breaking down information into its smallest meaningful parts! No more comma-separated lists or multiple values in one cell.

Key Rules of 1NF:

- Each table cell must contain a single value (atomic values)
- Each column must have a unique name
- Values in each column must be of the same type
- Each record must be unique (no duplicate rows)

### Messy school data



**Problems with this table**

- Courses column contains multiple values
- Multiple phone numbers in one cell
- Teacher information is combined and not atomic

### Neat, atomic data



**Improvements after 1NF**

- Each cell now contains a single value (atomic)
- Phone numbers are stored in a separate table, one per row
- Course enrollments are separated, with one course per row
- Each table has a clear purpose and all columns contain the same type of data
- We can easily add or remove phone numbers without affecting other data
- Course enrollments are now clearly linked to both students and teachers
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
